hook: You already know how to work with numbers and equations — now imagine organizing entire systems of data and transformations into compact, powerful grids called matrices. The gap is this: individual numbers can only take you so far, but matrices let you solve dozens of equations at once, model 3D rotations, and power the algorithms behind machine learning. What if a single operation could encode an entire transformation of space?
concept: This unit builds matrices from the ground up as mathematical objects you can manipulate, combine, and analyze — unifying the idea that matrices are not just data tables but representations of linear transformations whose structure is revealed through operations, inverses, and determinants.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to add matrices and scale them by constants, establishing the foundational arithmetic that all further matrix work depends on.
Master the row-times-column rule for multiplying matrices and understand why this non-commutative operation encodes the composition of linear transformations.
You will learn to flip a matrix across its diagonal and apply key properties of the transpose, including how it interacts with products and symmetric matrices.
Determine when a matrix has an inverse, compute it using row reduction, and use $A^{-1}$ to solve matrix equations efficiently.
Apply elementary matrices to formalize row operations and factor a matrix into lower and upper triangular components $A = LU$ for fast, reusable solving.
You will learn to compute the determinant of a matrix and use its properties to quickly test invertibility and measure how a transformation scales area or volume.
Apply cofactor expansion to evaluate determinants of any size and use Cramer's Rule to express solutions of linear systems explicitly in terms of determinants.
